title: PC Deals Weekly Update: August 3, 2003
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: A lot more deals appeared in this week's flyers from Best Buy and Circuit City, finally hailing the back-to-school buying frenzy. While HP, Sony, and eMachines all had multiple bundled offers, Compaq only had one desktop deal. We noticed an increase in Compaq's pricing on customized desktops at www.hpshopping.com, especially on Compaq monitors.
importance_rationale: Contemporaneous Kastner commentary tracking the 2002-2003 post-bubble PC price war in real time; paired with the access aggregate dataset for the same period.
relevance_rationale: Primary-source expert commentary on retail PC market dynamics; valuable for historians of the Wintel/AMD price war and consumer tech retail consolidation.
prescience_rationale: Near-term forecasts (next-month Intel price cuts, HT adoption, form-factor shifts) can be verified against subsequent weeks in the series.

title: PC Deals Weekly Update: August 10, 2003
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: This week launched the back-to-school season deals we've been expecting. Ads from Best Buy, Circuit City, Staples, CompUSA, and HP appeared in the Boston Globe. Not surprisingly, Best Buy and Circuit City led the pack with the most numerous and best priced offers.
importance_rationale: Contemporaneous Kastner commentary tracking the 2002-2003 post-bubble PC price war in real time; paired with the access aggregate dataset for the same period.
relevance_rationale: Primary-source expert commentary on retail PC market dynamics; valuable for historians of the Wintel/AMD price war and consumer tech retail consolidation.
prescience_rationale: Near-term forecasts (next-month Intel price cuts, HT adoption, form-factor shifts) can be verified against subsequent weeks in the series.

title: PC Deals Weekly Update: August 17, 2003
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: Last week was a great time to customize a new desktop at www.dell.com. This week, Dimension desktop prices jolted upwards in some cases over $200. But this doesn't mean that other online retailers that allow customization have increased their prices.
importance_rationale: Contemporaneous Kastner commentary tracking the 2002-2003 post-bubble PC price war in real time; paired with the access aggregate dataset for the same period.
relevance_rationale: Primary-source expert commentary on retail PC market dynamics; valuable for historians of the Wintel/AMD price war and consumer tech retail consolidation.
prescience_rationale: Near-term forecasts (next-month Intel price cuts, HT adoption, form-factor shifts) can be verified against subsequent weeks in the series.

title: PC Deals Weekly Update: August 24, 2003
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: Gateway lowered its prices on its 500S, 500X, and 500XL desktops by $100, making these models much more competitive. We suspect this much needed drop in Gateway's desktop prices comes as Dell announces price cuts ranging from 6% in consumer desktops to 22% for business servers. We were not sure how Dell's price cuts would be reflected in the Dimension models we configure for Pc Deals as Dell's prices have fluctuated in the past weeks.
importance_rationale: Contemporaneous Kastner commentary tracking the 2002-2003 post-bubble PC price war in real time; paired with the access aggregate dataset for the same period.
relevance_rationale: Primary-source expert commentary on retail PC market dynamics; valuable for historians of the Wintel/AMD price war and consumer tech retail consolidation.
prescience_rationale: Near-term forecasts (next-month Intel price cuts, HT adoption, form-factor shifts) can be verified against subsequent weeks in the series.

title: About Aberdeen Weekly PC Deals
author: Caroline S. Kastner
abstract: Program documentation for Aberdeen Group's Weekly PC Deals Section, describing methodology for tracking online retailers' lowest prices on name-brand PCs from Compaq, Dell, eMachines, Gateway, HP, and Sony. Defines nine price-point targets from $550 to $1,550+ reflecting the mass-market U.S. consumer PC segment, with weekly winner selection based on processor speed, memory, hard drive, optical drives, and bundled components.
importance_rationale: Formal program documentation anchoring the Aberdeen Weekly PC Deals dataset series; establishes measurement methodology for a 2002-2003 DCT tracking franchise.
relevance_rationale: Methodology description remains relevant to retail-price-tracking practitioners, though price points and vendor mix are dated.
prescience_rationale: Program-documentation file; contains no forward-looking predictions.

title: Digital Consumer Electronics and Information Technology — Staff Observations
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: Internal Aberdeen memo on the Digital Consumer Electronics and Information Technology (DCEIT) sector at a Q2-2002 inflection: Intel's $400M revenue shortfall, aggressive CPU price cuts, and AMD's 22% share gains driving PC OEM repositioning. Documents retail observations from Caroline Kastner's tracking research and catalogs vendor-specific line moves at Gateway and Dell ahead of the 2002 back-to-school season.
importance_rationale: Contemporaneous staff memo synthesizing Intel's Q2 2002 warning with retail PC observations; pairs CPU-supply dynamics with consumer-price execution.
relevance_rationale: Memo captures the precise mechanics of the PC price war; relevant as a case study in chip/retail coupling.
prescience_rationale: Kastner's read that Intel's May-26 cuts plus AMD 22% share would reset PC pricing through Q3-Q4 proved accurate; P4 2.26GHz did move to mainstream.

title: Digital Consumer Technology Supplier List (2002)
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: Aberdeen's four-category taxonomy of the Digital Consumer Technology (DCT) supplier landscape as of early 2002, spanning PCs & peripherals, networks & automation, applications & services, and leisure & entertainment. Catalogs 150+ named suppliers organized by the analytical framework used in Aberdeen's DCT tracking program.
importance_rationale: Snapshot of Aberdeen's DCT universe definition at the program's inception; anchors interpretation of subsequent weekly tracking files.
relevance_rationale: Historical record of the early-2000s consumer-tech vendor landscape; useful reference for studying market consolidation since.
prescience_rationale: Taxonomy document; contains no forward-looking predictions.

title: Microsoft Rolls Out Media Center
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: News analysis of Microsoft's announcement of Windows XP Media Center Edition — a living-room PC variant combining TV tuner, hardware video encoder, and infrared remote, first demoed at CES 2002 under the 'Freestyle' codename. HP, NEC, and Samsung are the launch OEMs; Kastner frames the offering as the strongest PC-in-the-living-room push to date while noting Windows task-scheduling vulnerabilities and competition from SonicBlue and TiVo.
importance_rationale: Documents the 2002 launch of Windows XP Media Center Edition — a seminal step toward convergence of PC and living-room entertainment.
relevance_rationale: Foundational context for home-theater-PC and later streaming-box trajectories; still referenced in PC convergence histories.
prescience_rationale: Kastner flagged Media Center as the 'strongest push to date' into the living room; multi-vendor MCE PC adoption followed through 2003-2008 and seeded today's HTPC and streaming-device market.

title: PC Replacements: Lawyers, Auditors, and Common Sense Rules
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: Aberdeen InSight arguing that the roughly 50 million Windows 98 / NT 4 corporate PCs still in service by mid-2003 should be accelerated off the desktop. Windows NT 4.x end-of-support (June 30, 2003) and Windows 98/SE (January 16, 2004) expose enterprises to unpatched security risk, auditor scrutiny, and potential negligence claims. Mid-2003 is framed as an auspicious PC replacement window anchored by Windows XP Pro SP1, Server 2003, Office 2003, Intel Springdale, Pentium 4 Hyper-Threading, and Centrino notebooks.
importance_rationale: Framed the mid-2003 enterprise PC replacement cycle and contributed to Aberdeen's CIO guidance during the Windows 98/NT 4 end-of-support transition.
relevance_rationale: Core framework (vendor support lifecycle + audit/legal exposure) remains directly applicable to enterprise OS migration planning today.
prescience_rationale: Kastner predicted (all proved correct): Windows XP Pro SP1 stability, Pentium 4 Hyper-Threading mainstreaming in 2003, sub-5-lb Centrino notebook adoption, Springdale 800 MHz FSB mainstreaming, ~1/3 corporate purchases going mobile.

title: nVidia Personal Cinema 1.0 — Aberdeen Lab Report
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: Aberdeen Group laboratory feedback report to nVidia management on 16 hours of hands-on testing of the Personal Cinema 1.0 kit (Compro GeForce2 MX 400 graphics card with VIVO breakout box) on a Dell 8100 running Windows XP. Evaluates graphics, TV tuner via WinDVR, DVD playback via WinDVD, Ulead Video Wave 4, and Microsoft Movie Maker workflows, and recommends packaging, cabling, and software-install improvements.
importance_rationale: Primary-source vendor-directed lab feedback documenting early-2002 state of consumer PC video/capture integration under Windows XP.
relevance_rationale: Useful historical reference for consumer DVR/HTPC integration usability — specifics dated but workflow observations remain instructive.
prescience_rationale: Correctly predicted (a) that consumers would click-through PCfriendly install warnings (broadly true across DRM-tied video), and (b) that Movie Maker ease-of-use 'must be reckoned with' — MS Movie Maker did become the lowest-friction consumer editor of the era.

title: nVidia GeForce FX Product-Line Briefing Notes
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: Briefing notes from an nVidia product-line meeting with Brian Burke and Todd Reddick covering the GeForce FX NV30/NV31/NV34 lineup — targets, process (TSMC 0.13 micron / 0.15 micron), memory (DDR1/DDR2), AGP 8x, DirectX 9 support, NV35 high-end future, and SKU positioning versus ATI Radeon 9500 and prior GeForce Ti 4200/MX 440 parts. Includes pricing of $99 (NV34) to $399 (NV30/5800) and April ship timing.
importance_rationale: Primary-source contemporaneous notes from an analyst briefing — useful artifact for GPU-lineup historians.
relevance_rationale: Provides early-2003 competitive framing of nVidia vs ATI across mainstream/low-end GPU tiers.
prescience_rationale: Lineup ship dates and price points largely materialized, though NV30 (5800) proved disappointing vs ATI 9700 and was rapidly refreshed by NV35 (5900).

title: Integrating HP and Compaq: Success Must Stem from the PC Business
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: Aberdeen InSight arguing that the newly announced HP/Compaq merger's success will be determined by the PC business — not by divesting it. Frames the combined $29B 'access devices' segment (desktops, notebooks, iPaq/Jornada PDAs) as strategic for server-component sharing and printer/consumer bundling. Forecasts the merged entity will hold 19% global PC share (#1 position, 5-6 points ahead of Dell) and flags consumer IT as the key battleground for Carly Fiorina's stated #1-consumer-IT strategy.
importance_rationale: One of Kastner's most widely-read post-announcement calls on the HP/Compaq merger; explicitly frames the PC-business thesis that later proved correct.
relevance_rationale: Core case study in IT megamerger analysis; still cited in PC-industry consolidation retrospectives.
prescience_rationale: Kastner predicted (correctly) that HP would retain the PC business and combine at #1 global share; Fiorina's consumer-IT strategy, iPaq market leadership, and Windows-PDA displacement of Palm all materialized by 2003-2005.

title: Video Product Segmentation — Aberdeen DCT Market Map
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: Aberdeen DCT market-segmentation map of consumer PC video products across four feature categories: (1) digital/analog video capture, (2) TV-input to PC monitor, (3) TV-output to TV/stereo, and (4) combined TV + graphics card. Lists hardware and software suppliers per category with indicative pricing ($80 ATI TV Wonder up to $500 Radeon 8500DV) and captures the 2002 state of PC/TV convergence peripherals.
importance_rationale: Taxonomy snapshot of the 2002 PC video peripheral market; supports interpretation of subsequent DCT weekly files and lab reports.
relevance_rationale: Historical reference for the PC/TV convergence peripherals market; obsolete but illustrative of category definitions.
prescience_rationale: Taxonomy/product-segmentation document; contains no forward-looking predictions.

title: Personal Information Technology — Aberdeen Practice Launch
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: Program-launch description of Aberdeen Group's Personal Information Technology practice, established by Peter Kastner in 2001. Articulates the nine market paradoxes driving the practice (PC commoditization, digital-device convergence, home-network emergence, 3G wireless, digital TV transition, in-home services needs), catalogs service offerings, technology segments, strategic market questions, key market trends, and the supplier coverage list (Microsoft, Intel, AMD, Compaq, Dell, HP, IBM, Sony, Toshiba, Apple, Gateway, Micron, AOL, Epson, Lexmark, Canon, Nvidia, Cirrus Logic).
importance_rationale: Foundational documentation of Aberdeen's Personal IT practice — establishes the analytical framework under which subsequent DCT, weekly PC Deals, and lab-report work is produced.
relevance_rationale: Primary-source description of a research program still referenced in IT industry-analyst histories; relevant for understanding Kastner's 2001-2005 output.
prescience_rationale: Prescient framing — the practice's nine drivers (PC commoditization, home networks, digital TV, 3G wireless, digital photography, multi-PC households, digital appliances, MP3-replacing-cassette, broadband last-mile) map closely to the 2002-2008 consumer-tech reality.

title: HP's Consumer IT Leadership Needs More Focus On Consumers (Sep 5 2001 draft)
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: September 5, 2001 Aberdeen Lab InSight draft on HP's consumer IT execution, based on hands-on experience with HP's C315 digital camera. Earlier version of the 'Caveat Emptor' InSight later published December 5, 2001. Documents the Windows XP upgrade-in-place compatibility failure caused by HP's bundled photo-transfer software interfering with Windows XP native camera support — and the 45-minute support call that cost HP more than the camera's retail price per Aberdeen help-desk research.
importance_rationale: Earlier draft of Kastner's HP consumer-IT critique; provides drafting provenance but analytical content substantially duplicates the Dec 5 2001 final.
relevance_rationale: Specific HP C315 issues are dated; the broader 'Windows XP compatibility + bundled software interference' pattern remains relevant.
prescience_rationale: Predicted 'many unhappy HP camera owners' on Windows XP upgrade — pattern of bundled-software vs native-OS-support conflicts indeed recurred industry-wide.

title: Microsoft HomeStation — Analyst Briefing Notes
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: Briefing notes on Microsoft's HomeStation concept — a home-server platform bridging traditional PC with home stereo and TV, built on Xbox hardware with Windows XP 2.0 (codename Longwood) replacing Windows 2000, Terminal Server support to drive Mira tablets and remote controls, and nVidia TV-tuner integration. Freestyle is the HomeStation UI (demoed at CES 2002). Frames Microsoft as a $1.5B hardware player via mice, WebTV, and Xbox — and HomeStation as a stealth path to become a major PC vendor.
importance_rationale: Primary-source contemporaneous notes on Microsoft's living-room / home-server strategy ahead of the public Media Center PC launch (August 2002); captures strategic framing before it was public.
relevance_rationale: Living-room-PC strategy is well-documented historically; notes add texture on Mira/Xbox/Terminal Server architectural choices.
prescience_rationale: Correctly framed Microsoft's multi-hardware push (Xbox + Mira + Media Center convergence) as stealth PC-vendor strategy; this materialized through Media Center PC, Xbox Media Extenders, and later Xbox as a media hub.

title: DCT Segment Priorities Matrix (Home / Work / Mobile)
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: Aberdeen Personal IT / DCT segment-priorities matrix cross-tabulating four technology categories (Computers & Peripherals, Networking, Productivity Apps & Services, Leisure & Entertainment) against three contexts (Home, Work, Mobile). Captures priority rankings with analyst notes — including desktop-CPU 2.2->3.6 GHz 18-month roadmap, Pentium 4 laptop impact, 2002 as a 'banner year for home networks' caveated on wireless usability, and 2003 PC/TV/stereo integration outlook.
importance_rationale: Internal prioritization framework used to drive Aberdeen Personal IT coverage decisions; anchors interpretation of subsequent topic/weekly output.
relevance_rationale: Matrix structure remains relevant to any multi-segment tech analyst practice; specific technology calls are dated.
prescience_rationale: Kastner's 2.2→3.6 GHz desktop CPU call in 18 months was directionally correct (mainstream P4 reached 3.6 GHz by 2004); WiFi usability + Pentium 4 laptop inflection both materialized in 2003.

title: Adobe Systems Inc. — Aberdeen DCT Company Snapshot
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: Short Aberdeen DCT company-snapshot on Adobe Systems, covering corporate facts (founded 1982, second-largest PC software company, 345 Park Avenue San Jose), product categories (content design/production/publishing; digital video; digital imaging; document rendering including fonts, print rendering, ePaper), and executive roster (Warnock, Geschke as co-chairmen; Chizen CEO; Demo CFO; Lamkin SVP Graphics; Eschbach VP ePaper).
importance_rationale: Short company-profile card within Aberdeen DCT coverage set; limited analytical content; likely a template-in-progress.
relevance_rationale: Historical reference only; executive roster and product-category descriptions rapidly dated.
prescience_rationale: Company-profile card; contains no forward-looking predictions.

title: Personal IT Forecast Research Compendium (2000-2001)
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: A curated research compendium assembled by Peter S. Kastner capturing key PC and semiconductor market forecasts from Dataquest, IDC, Merrill Lynch, TechWeb, and Japanese industry sources during the 2000-2001 PC downturn. Covers worldwide PC shipment declines, U.S. market contraction, DRAM price collapse, Japan/Asia growth, and Kastner's own dissent [PSK disagrees] on Rambus DRAM demand expectations. Source material for Aberdeen's Personal IT practice forecasting work.
importance_rationale: Captures the primary-source forecast inputs (Dataquest, IDC, Merrill Lynch) Kastner used to launch Aberdeen's Personal IT practice in 2001; includes his own editorial dissent on Rambus demand.
relevance_rationale: Provides a contemporaneous record of how analysts framed the first-ever PC shipment decline -- directly relevant to studying analyst accuracy and tech market inflection points.
prescience_rationale: Embedded forecasts (e.g., Dataquest 2005 chip revenue of $299.85B) proved broadly accurate in direction; Rambus DRAM mainstream adoption did not occur, partially validating PSK's skepticism.

title: Why Aberdeen Is Following Consumer PC Deals — DCT Practice Methodology
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: Methodology paper explaining Aberdeen's Digital Consumer Technology practice approach to weekly consumer PC price tracking. Defines the research questions, six tracked brands (Compaq, Dell, eMachines, Gateway, HP, Sony), an 8-factor price-value hierarchy (CPU, memory, modem/NIC, HDD, monitor, OS, printer, software), and weekly commentary format. Acknowledges 90 days of prior tracking; explicitly excludes Apple (no head-to-head competition).
importance_rationale: Foundational methodology document underpinning dozens of subsequent weekly PC Deals commentaries; defines the Aberdeen-Kastner price-value framework.
relevance_rationale: Price-value tradeoff framework remains relevant to consumer tech tracking; specific hardware thresholds (128MB RAM, 40GB HDD) are dated but useful as 2002 benchmarks.
prescience_rationale: Framework correctly anticipated rebate-driven price adjustment mechanics and HP/Compaq dual-brand challenges post-merger.

title: Digital Consumer Technology: The Revolution Has Started — Aberdeen Webinar
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: Promotional announcement for a 1-hour Aberdeen webinar on Nov 1, 2002 featuring Peter Kastner (EVP/CRO), Russ Craig (Research Director, Semiconductors), and Isaac Ro (Research Associate, Emerging Technologies). Three topics: mobile convergence devices, home media servers, and home networking challenges. Frames DCT as an arena of blurring product boundaries and emergent firms.
importance_rationale: Marks a formal Aberdeen DCT practice public deliverable; captures Kastner's 2002 framing of DCT as a converging category.
relevance_rationale: Topical framing (mobile convergence, media servers, home networking) presaged the smartphone/streaming/mesh-WiFi eras.
prescience_rationale: Mobile convergence devices (smartphones), home media servers (cloud streaming), and home networking (mesh WiFi) all became mainstream as predicted.

title: Personal Information Technology (PIT) Service Offering — Tiered Pricing
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: Internal/sales-facing tiered service offering for Aberdeen's Personal Information Technology practice. Defines target company classes (CE, computer, cable equipment, WiFi-x, HomePNA, retail, ILECs), five core category slides (Gateways, Media Servers/PC-CE convergence, Networks, Displays/UI/Usability, Internet/Cable enablement), and three pricing tiers: $19,500 Basic, $24,500 Basic+Contenders, and $24,500 + $4,000/diem Custom Consulting.
importance_rationale: Internal commercial artifact showing the commercial structure supporting Aberdeen's DCT/PIT research; useful for understanding industry-analyst-firm business models.
relevance_rationale: Tiered-retainer model remains standard industry-analyst-firm commercial structure today.
prescience_rationale: Pricing document, not a forecast.

title: Aberdeen PITAS (Personal Information Technology Awareness Service) 2002 Agreement Template
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: Contract template (with <NAME><COMPANY> placeholders) for Aberdeen's Personal Information Technology Awareness Service (PITAS) 2002 program at $2,500/year. Deliverables: Supplier Snapshot on Aberdeen website, objective press quotes, analyst reference for press/investors, and bi-annual one-hour briefings. Principal analysts: Peter Kastner, Ethan Cohen, Isaac Ro. Includes renewal, reprint fees ($1/reprint), and legal liability provisions.
importance_rationale: Artifact of the light-touch PITAS tier within Aberdeen's PIT practice; documents the commercial entry-point for vendor awareness at $2,500/yr.
relevance_rationale: Specific template contents are largely obsolete but informative for commercial history of industry-analyst awareness services.
prescience_rationale: Contract template, no predictions.

title: Personal Electronic Technology Vision — Sony Client Presentation
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: Strategy/vision deck prepared for Sony (V4.30 V2) covering personal electronic technology used at home, in transit, and at work over a 3+ year horizon. Articulates a Plug-and-Play imperative, upgradability, and customer-support model; enumerates home electronics (gateway, entertainment server, displays, game box, MP3 player, camera, laptop, PDA, Mira), work mobility, and play/telematics (Bluetooth/WiFi/cellular/GPS/rear-seat entertainment). Implications for Sony: competitive threats, what Sony must get right, and business process/channel issues.
importance_rationale: Direct Kastner/Aberdeen strategy advice to Sony captured in a single document; unusual first-person client-facing artifact.
relevance_rationale: Framework of home/transit/work product zones and the Plug-and-Play imperative remains directly relevant to consumer tech product strategy.
prescience_rationale: Predicted convergence of phone/answering/VOIP, wireless MP3, pocket PC, telematics (GPS+cellular+WiFi) + drive-by WiFi location services -- all realized via smartphone/connected-car.

title: Aberdeen Group Semiconductor Research Practice Definition (2002)
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: Formal definition of Aberdeen Group's Semiconductor Research Practice: focus on enabling semiconductor technologies for emerging digital markets (MEMS, photonics, software-defined radios, NPUs, UWB), a taxonomy of semiconductor ecosystem trends (design, fabrication, test), and detailed target account lists spanning 36+ firms (Agere, AMD, ARM, ATI, Broadcom, IBM, Intel, Nvidia, Samsung, Sony, TSMC, etc.). Captures the 2001 semiconductor downturn context (-32% to $132B WW revenue) and a forward-looking view of fabless trends, design/NRE cost inflation ($10M+ per device), 300mm facility cost ($2.7B), verification engineering dominance (70% of cycle), DFT, SoC test, and molecular/Plastic-Logic disruption candidates.
importance_rationale: Defines the charter of a major Aberdeen practice; documents Kastner-era strategic framing of semiconductor industry structure at a key 2001-2002 inflection point.
relevance_rationale: Concepts (fabless, foundries, DFT, SoC test, 300mm, sub-100nm, MEMS, photonics) remain directly relevant to semiconductor industry today.
prescience_rationale: Correctly predicted (i) only TSMC/Intel/Samsung would fund leading-edge internally, (ii) growing fabless share, (iii) DFT mainstreaming, (iv) SOI adoption, (v) photonics/MEMS need for new test methods. Plastic Logic did not scale commercially.

title: Peter Kastner's Wish List
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: Kastner's personal 2002 holiday wish list of six digital consumer technology products: HP Scanjet 5500c, Dell Axim X5 Pocket PC, Viewsonic NextVision N6 PC-HDTV bridge, Gateway Profile 4 XL all-in-one, nVidia GeForce FX graphics card, and Davis Instruments Vantage Pro Plus weather station. Each item is framed as both a consumer product and an industry signal.
importance_rationale: Personal wish list format, but captures the 2002 DCT zeitgeist: Pocket PC/WiFi convergence, PC-HDTV bridges, all-in-one PC renaissance, GPU innovation race, and consumer sensor networks.
relevance_rationale: Item categories (Pocket PC, all-in-one PC, PC-display HDTV, GPU, home weather sensors) remain relevant product categories; specific models long EOL.
prescience_rationale: Correctly identified Gateway Profile 4 industrial design direction, GPU importance for future Windows UI (Vista Aero delivered 2006), and PDA/phone convergence trajectory. PC-as-HDTV-source (NextVision N6) did not scale.

title: Analysis of Intel Processor Prices in PC Deals (January 2003)
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: Analysis of Intel Celeron and Pentium 4 processor price tiers observed in Aberdeen's PC Deals tracking from August 2002 through January 2003. Documents the bifurcation of P4 into 400 MHz and 533 MHz front-side-bus families, the introduction of hyperthreading 3.06 GHz P4, and the mechanics of older CPUs being pushed down price bands by new releases. Predicts near-term obsolescence of the P4 2.0 GHz.
importance_rationale: Rare contemporaneous analysis of Intel CPU price-band dynamics during a pivotal transition (hyperthreading launch and 533 MHz FSB adoption) using Kastner's PC Deals retail tracking.
relevance_rationale: Historical methodology example of price-tier analysis in the PC retail channel; specific CPUs long EOL.
prescience_rationale: Correctly predicted the 2.0 GHz P4 obsolescence and the divergent pricing of 400 vs 533 MHz FSB families; called the start of a new high-end $2200-$3000 tier around the 3.06 GHz hyperthreading P4.

title: Traveling With Centrino
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: Hands-on review after two months of travel with a Gateway 450 Intel Centrino notebook. Covers the Pentium M + chipset + WiFi Centrino platform architecture, 15in 1400x1050 screen trade-offs, 6.2lb weight versus preferred thin-and-light 4lb class, 1.5 GHz performance versus Pentium III/P4 desktops, battery life including DVD playback plus work day, WiFi range improvements including a Cometa McDonald's hotspot picked up from a 15th-floor hotel room across the street, and corporate 3-notebooks-per-7-desktops adoption forecast.
importance_rationale: Contemporaneous first-person field test of Intel's $300M Centrino launch platform at the moment it entered corporate notebook rollouts; pairs vendor narrative with real travel use.
relevance_rationale: Thin-and-light versus desktop-replacement trade-off, battery/WiFi use patterns, and mobile productivity framing remain directly applicable; specific hardware dated.
prescience_rationale: Correctly forecast Centrino becoming mainstream for business laptops and WiFi infrastructure rapid growth in hotels and meeting spaces; called thin-and-light/tablet/executive-performance variants that Intel shipped.

title: Apple Ships Hot PowerMac G5
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: Hot Topic piece on Apple's Power Mac G5 launch. Analyzes SPEC CPU 2000 benchmark claims (G5 2.0 GHz beats Xeon and P4 in floating point, loses integer; dual-G5 beats both in integer and floating point), the 64-bit marketing claim relative to Alpha/HP-PA/PowerPC/Sun/Itanium 2 predecessors, the $3998 configuration with ATI 9800 Pro and Apple 17in Studio Display, PCI-X and 8x AGP I/O, USB/FireWire 800/gigabit Ethernet. Concludes G5 is legitimate workstation competitor but an Apple-base renovation rather than a PC-attack product.
importance_rationale: Immediate Aberdeen analysis of Apple's most aggressive Mac performance positioning in years, at the dawn of Apple's transition from PowerPC to x86 (2005-2006) that this piece presaged through its Intel-comparison framing.
relevance_rationale: Workstation versus high-end-PC positioning and Mac-specific productivity arguments (PhotoShop gains) remain relevant templates for Mac-vs-PC analysis.
prescience_rationale: Correctly predicted G5 renovates Apple base more than attacks PC segment; recognized 64-bit was not first (Alpha, PA, PowerPC, Sun, Itanium 2 preceded); 2-yr later Apple transitioned to Intel, ending PowerPC era.

title: Gateway's Acquisition of eMachines Bodes Well
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: Aberdeen analysis of Gateway's $235 million acquisition of privately-held eMachines. Argues the deal is a shrewd distribution-channel play: eMachines's shelf space at Best Buy, Circuit City, and CompUSA lets Gateway deemphasize its costly 170-store chain, combine Gateway's high-end all-in-ones and plasma TVs with eMachines's budget retail strength to out-compete HP, and expand digital consumer electronics distribution.
importance_rationale: Contemporary real-time Aberdeen call on one of the most consequential mid-2000s PC industry consolidations, including the sharp reading that Gateway would exit its own showroom chain.
relevance_rationale: Case study of distribution-channel-as-acquisition-rationale remains directly relevant to consumer hardware M&A analysis.
prescience_rationale: Correctly forecast Gateway exiting its 170 captive showrooms (announced closures within months of deal close Mar 2004). Gateway ultimately acquired by Acer 2007; eMachines retired as brand 2013.

title: PC Management Best Practices: A Study of the Total Cost of Operation, Risk, Security, and Audit
author: Mark Salamasick, Charles Le Grand
abstract: IIA Research Foundation rough-draft report (Oct 7, 2003) authored by Mark Salamasick (University of Texas at Dallas) and Charles Le Grand (IIA AVP Technology Practices). Based on 221 GAIN flash-survey responses plus the July 2003 IIA Technology Audit Forum where Peter Kastner of Aberdeen Group was keynote speaker. Organized around the COSO Internal Control Integrated Framework and SAC model; covers business risk and insurance, Sarbanes-Oxley/HIPAA/GLBA compliance, information security and appropriate use, business continuity, software management, and PC TCO. Cites Kastner's research extensively on PC fleet TCO ($8k-$9k per seat), 20-25% cost reduction through best practices, 70-80% of TCO in operations, 40 million desktops running outdated OSes, and standardized images.
importance_rationale: Landmark IIA Research Foundation publication that codified PC fleet audit practices on the eve of the Sarbanes-Oxley IT-controls wave; heavily features Kastner's Aberdeen Group research as keynote input.
relevance_rationale: COSO-structured PC controls, auditor role in PC fleet management, TCO methodology, and patch/image/standardization best practices remain foundational for IT audit and endpoint management practice.
prescience_rationale: Correctly framed Sarbanes-Oxley's demand for documented PC controls, anticipated wireless risk (only 21% had done wireless cost analysis at time of writing), flagged old-OS security crisis (Win95/98/NT4), and identified patch management as dominant risk vector (~90% of attacks target known vulnerabilities).

title: Digital Consumer Electronic and Information Technology (DCEIT) Practice — v6 Overview Deck
author: Russ Craig, Peter S. Kastner
abstract: Sixth-revision Aberdeen DCEIT practice overview deck covering PC-CE convergence, six competitor categories (broadband, CE point-product, game, PC/peripheral, display, software, new entrants), Aberdeen's research approach, fundamental principles governing the convergence, named vendor products (Motorola DCP501/BMC 8000/BMC 9000, Scientific Atlanta Explorer 8000, Pace Micro Home Media Center, Thomson ADSL Gateway, Netopia, 2Wire, Intel reference designs, Broadcom, ESS, Wind River Tornado), and implications for CE companies across channels, supply chain, and customer support. Final v6 iteration of a deck evolved through DCT-Board-Presentation (Jul 2002), DCT-practice-jul-26 (Jul 26 2002), and DCEIT-Practice (Aug-Sep 2002). Craig is Research Director for Semiconductors and DCEIT; Kastner is EVP/CRO overseeing the DCT practice.
importance_rationale: Canonical positioning document for Aberdeen's DCEIT/DCT practice; defined the six-category player taxonomy Aberdeen used through 2003-2004 and predicted convergence dynamics that materialized over the following decade.
relevance_rationale: PC-CE convergence thesis matured into smart TV, streaming appliances, and connected home — framework remains useful for retrospective industry analysis; specific vendor detail dated.
prescience_rationale: Predicted CE-PC convergence, commoditization of box-level products, displacement of incumbent CE business models by Apex Digital-style rapid-cycle Chinese-manufactured alternatives, Sony/Microsoft/Nintendo game platform centrality, and blue-laser/UWB timelines — all largely confirmed.

title: Digital Consumer Technology: The Revolution Has Started — Aberdeen DCT Webinar Deck
author: Russ Craig, Peter S. Kastner, Isaac Ro
abstract: Presentation slides from Aberdeen Group's November 1, 2002 DCT webinar. Three deep-dive sections: Mobile Convergence Devices (handset economics, network operator gatekeepers, 3G timing), Media Servers (desired functionality, emerging product classes from HP Media Center/Intel Media PC/Sony Cocoon/STB accessories, Media Center requirements), and Home Networking (eleven contending technologies, longer-term architecture with consumer-configurable gateway/firewall/VoIP/DRM, 5 GHz spectrum of choice). Closes with DCT Practice Services retainer package and contact for Peter Kastner. Complements the companion DCEIT v6 practice deck (dct-dceit-practice-v6-2002) and the pre-webinar promotional announcement (dct-webinar-announcement-2002-11).
importance_rationale: First formal Aberdeen DCT public webinar deliverable; concrete vendor-level evidence (HP, Intel, Sony, Microsoft reference designs) for the mobile/media-server/home-networking predictions that became mainstream over 2003-2015.
relevance_rationale: Topical framing (mobile convergence, home media servers, home networking) materialized as smartphones, streaming STBs/smart TVs, and mesh Wi-Fi — specific vendor detail dated.
prescience_rationale: Predicted PC makers competing with phone makers, handset life-cycle compression via contract manufacturing, 3G 3+ years out (true — US 3G rollout peaked 2005-2006), MMS and gaming as 'What's Hot' drivers, media-server consolidation replacing disk duplication, and 5 GHz becoming spectrum of choice — all confirmed.

title: Memoir: Prescience Call on the AMD Athlon 64 — Reassessing the Aug 2003 PC Deals Commentary
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: Retrospective memoir by Peter S. Kastner (April 2026) assessing the prescience of Aberdeen PC Deals weekly commentary published Aug 3, 10, 17, and 24 2003 — the four weeks immediately preceding the AMD Athlon 64 launch on Sept 23, 2003. Those weeklies continued to recommend Athlon XP (the A220n/A230n HP desktops and eMachines T2605) as the value play without anticipating the 64-bit architectural transition. Kastner's retrospective view: AMD was 'way early' on 64-bit — Windows XP x64 Edition did not ship until April 2005, and mainstream 64-bit consumer applications trailed by several more years. For an August 2003 consumer buyer on a typical 3-4 year PC lifecycle (through 2006-2007), 64-bit was not a compelling purchase factor at time of purchase and arguably was still not compelling at end-of-life. Net judgment: the commentary's silence on Athlon 64 was a forecasting gap, but the Athlon XP recommendation was defensible ex post for the consumer-value use case it addressed. Prescience scored 'medium' — missed signal, non-material to the consumer who acted on the recommendation.
importance_rationale: Documents how a reasoned consumer-value recommendation can diverge from a forecasting-miss without producing harm to the buyer; illustrates the difference between market-timing prescience and consumer-utility prescience.
relevance_rationale: The analytical frame (separating 'correct call for the buyer' from 'accurate forecast of the industry inflection') is reusable for evaluating any contemporaneous technology recommendation against later architectural transitions — AI accelerators, Apple Silicon, ARM servers, etc.
prescience_rationale: Retrospective itself correctly identifies that the 2003 silence was non-material: 64-bit Windows and 64-bit consumer app adoption ran 2005-2008, later than any 2003 purchase lifecycle mattered; assessment confirmed by the historical record.

title: RAMP vs DCT: A Methodology-Level Comparison of My Prescience Track Record (2026 Retrospective)
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: A 2026 retrospective memoir comparing Peter Kastner's 2001-2004 prescience track record across two Kastner-authored methodologies: the Maxtor RAMP structured-interview corpus (39 studies, 2-5 year enterprise horizons) and the DCT weekly consumer PC retail tracker (75 studies, days-to-weeks tactical horizons). Written after the Phase 3 backfill of 105 DCT DEFERRED observations, this memoir argues that the methodology differences — not analyst skill differences — drive the gap in study-level prescience scoring, and shows that at the observation level both corpuses post strong records.
importance_rationale: First synthesis across two Kastner-authored corpuses on a common grading footing post-Phase-3 backfill; establishes a methodology-level prescience comparison framework.
relevance_rationale: Methodology comparison (structured interviews vs tactical tracking) remains directly applicable to modern research and AI-cycle analysis.
prescience_rationale: Retrospective analysis of prior work; the memoir evaluates past predictions rather than making new ones.

title: 1999 Aberdeen Group Retainer Services Proposal to Computer Associates
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: November 20, 1998 Aberdeen Group retainer services proposal from Peter S. Kastner to Herb Siegel (VP Analyst Relations) at Computer Associates, proposing five Knowledge Center retainer subscriptions (Information Security, Internet Infrastructures, Application Development & SDM, Systems & Network Management, Electronic Commerce) plus Industry Services for calendar year 1999. Documents the commercial relationship structure between Aberdeen and CA at the height of the CA-Aberdeen analyst partnership.
importance_rationale: Primary-source commercial document of a major analyst-firm/client retainer structure in the late 1990s; documents Aberdeen's Knowledge Center catalog.
relevance_rationale: Historical interest for analyst-industry economics; less directly applicable today.
prescience_rationale: Commercial proposal document; not a predictive analysis.

title: PCs, NCs, and NetPCs — Where Do We Go From Here? (CA Acapulco CIO Conference 1997)
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: 'PCs, NCs, and NetPCs — Where Do We Go From Here?' presentation by Peter S. Kastner to the Computer Associates Acapulco CIO Conference in May 1997. Frames the late-1990s desktop dilemma between Microsoft/Intel (Wintel PC), Oracle/Sun (Network Computer / NC), and the Microsoft/Intel NetPC compromise. Analyzes desktop business drivers (intranets, extranets, JIT supply chain, globalization) and argues where each client architecture fits best.
importance_rationale: Captures the late-1990s desktop-architecture debate at its peak, delivered to the CA CIO audience the year Oracle/Sun first shipped the NC and Microsoft/Intel responded with NetPC.
relevance_rationale: Client-architecture framings are now well-settled history; the Wintel PC survived both NC and NetPC challenges.
prescience_rationale: Kastner framed the NC/NetPC debate correctly: NC and NetPC did not displace the fat-client PC in general enterprise desktop use; the arguments he highlighted (TCO, centralization, thin-client economics) re-emerged successfully a decade later as VDI, Chromebooks, and web-SaaS.

title: Customer Focused Selling — Computer Associates Sales Training Curriculum
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: Two-day Customer Focused Selling sales-training curriculum for Computer Associates, developed by Aberdeen Group. Day 1 covers behavior styles (DiSC), credibility/trust, communication, questioning technique, and customer-focused selling; Day 2 covers value proposition, price-vs-value, closing, and roleplay. The document is the structured course outline with time allocations per topic. Reflects Aberdeen's sales-force-education practice during the CA retainer era.
importance_rationale: Documents Aberdeen's sales-force-education practice and the DiSC-based methodology applied to enterprise-software sales training at CA.
relevance_rationale: Sales-training methodology framework remains applicable but DiSC-based adult-learning design is well-documented in other sources.
prescience_rationale: Training curriculum, not a predictive analysis.

title: IT Infrastructure: Are We Building Chaos or Cosmos? (CA-sponsored infrastructure keynote, 1998)
author: Peter S. Kastner
source_file: CA Infrastructure speech 1998 PK.pdf; Infrastructure speech.odp
abstract: 'IT Infrastructure: Are We Building Chaos or Cosmos?' — Peter S. Kastner keynote on enterprise IT infrastructure strategy delivered under Computer Associates' sponsorship circa 1998. Frames IT spending as ~3% of revenue with 85% going to ongoing operations and 15% to new applications; distinguishes CEO/CFO/CIO perspectives; argues for consolidating fragmented infrastructure through an 'IT cosmos' architectural program rather than piecemeal ('chaos') procurement.
importance_rationale: Canonical Kastner framing of IT-infrastructure strategy for the CA customer base at the peak of the pre-2000 enterprise-infrastructure build-out; introduces the 'chaos vs cosmos' architectural framing.
relevance_rationale: Ongoing-operations-vs-new-applications spending split (85/15) remains a useful baseline framing for CIO conversations; fragmented-infrastructure argument re-emerges every decade (cloud sprawl, AI sprawl).
prescience_rationale: Kastner's 85/15 ongoing-ops-vs-new-apps ratio has held up across decades as a stable IT-spending benchmark; the 'consolidate-or-fragment' framing reappears continuously in every subsequent IT-infrastructure cycle.

title: interBiz Solutions BizWorks Profile — Management Command and Control for the E-World
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: Aberdeen Group analyst profile (c. 1999) of interBiz Solutions, the new application software and services division of Computer Associates, and its BizWorks framework for cross-enterprise application integration. The profile positions BizWorks as the infrastructure that would tie best-of-breed applications and processes together, leveraging CA's Unicenter TNG management, Jasmine object database, 3D visualization, and Neugents pattern-recognition agents. Argues that enterprises should 'buy' BizWorks rather than 'make' equivalent integration themselves.
importance_rationale: Primary-source analyst profile of CA's interBiz/BizWorks strategy at launch; rare documentation of a short-lived but strategically important CA division created at the peak of the EAI/portal cycle.
relevance_rationale: interBiz and BizWorks did not persist as durable product lines; profile remains valuable as a history artifact of late-1990s EAI / pre-API-economy integration thinking.
prescience_rationale: BizWorks and interBiz failed to establish as durable product lines; Kastner's profile was optimistic about the make-vs-buy case and about Neugents adoption — neither claim held up as framed, though the underlying intellectual threads (predictive analytics, cross-enterprise integration) were directionally correct.

title: An Overview of Internet Application Development (CA Sales Training June 1996)
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: 'An Overview of Internet Application Development' — Peter S. Kastner presentation to Computer Associates Sales Training, June 1996. Introduces the Internet/intranet stack (thin-client browser, TCP/IP, HTTP, CGI, firewall, RDBMS) and contrasts Internet applications with traditional client-server. Early canonical analyst framing of the Web as an application-delivery platform delivered to a CA sales audience in the Netscape-dominant pre-IE3 era.
importance_rationale: Early (June 1996) analyst framing of Internet application development for a major enterprise-software sales force, delivered at the inflection point between client-server and Web architectures.
relevance_rationale: Content is foundational-education material; architectural contrasts remain interesting as a period piece.
prescience_rationale: Kastner's 1996 framing of Web apps ('runs on a server(s); presentation by client browser; no session') turned out to be the dominant enterprise-application architecture within five years; his 'no concept of a session' framing foreshadowed the later shift to stateless REST.

title: What Every CFO Should Know About the Year 2000 IT Tsunami (CA CFO Executive Exchange December 1997)
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: 'What Every CFO Should Know About the Year 2000 IT Tsunami' — Peter S. Kastner presentation to Computer Associates' CFO Executive Exchange, December 3-6, 1997. Argues that CFOs must treat Y2K as an existential business-continuity issue, with specific deadlines: Y2K-safe applications in place by September 30, 1998 for five quarters of testing/cutover; twelve-month renovation program started by September 30, 1997. Recommends customer/supplier audits, EDI-link testing, insurance review, and management-options (renovate/replace/outsource/retire) analysis.
importance_rationale: Primary-source analyst advice to CFOs on Y2K with explicit deadline math (9/30/98 applications in place; 9/30/97 start of 12-month program) and supply-chain/EDI audit recommendations.
relevance_rationale: Y2K is a settled historical event; the deadline-math framework remains a useful analytical template for infrastructure-transition programs.
prescience_rationale: Kastner's 9/30/98 deadline for Y2K-safe applications matched industry best-practice advice; the supply-chain/EDI audit recommendation correctly anticipated that Y2K risk was a network-effect issue, not just an internal-IT issue.

title: Letter to Sanjay Kumar on CA World 2001
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: Post-CA-World-2001 thank-you letter from Peter S. Kastner (Founder and Chief Research Officer, Aberdeen Group) to Sanjay Kumar (CEO, Computer Associates), dated July 19, 2001. Observations include: CA's analyst-relations team (Debra Cattani, Janice Thomas) as industry-leading; CA customer-satisfaction at a nine-year high; the 'CA animosity factor' reduced; press perception lagging reality; dismissive assessment of the 'Ranger situation' (Sam Wyly/Ranger Governance proxy contest); positive review of the 3x6 CA World branding framework.
importance_rationale: Primary-source communication from Aberdeen's Chief Research Officer to the CA CEO immediately before the August-2002 Kumar indictment era; captures Kastner's pre-scandal read of CA customer sentiment and the Ranger proxy contest.
relevance_rationale: Historically important as an on-the-record analyst read of CA weeks before the Wyly Ranger settlement and a year before Kumar's eventual departure.
prescience_rationale: Kastner read the CA-Ranger contest correctly ('non-starter with customers'; the Ranger campaign lost the 2001 proxy fight). Read the CA recovery correctly at a point when press sentiment still lagged.

title: Aberdeen Group Assessment of Unicenter and Jasmine for Computer Associates
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: Letter-form report from Peter S. Kastner (Group Vice President, Aberdeen Group) to David M. Hood (Director of Analyst Relations, Computer Associates) following a two-day analyst roundtable evaluation of CA's Unicenter and Jasmine products. Kastner delivers pointed positioning recommendations to maximize 1997 revenue, including brand-led advertising, ISV instrumentation, a VAR program, non-traditional environments, a national fault-response center, and competitive collateral against Tivoli. The letter also proposes a broad Aberdeen-CA consulting engagement.
importance_rationale: First-person Aberdeen-to-CA strategy letter capturing a pivotal 1996 moment for Unicenter and Jasmine when CA still held dominant share in enterprise systems management and Tivoli was ascendant under new IBM ownership.
relevance_rationale: Framework (branding, ISV instrumentation, partner channels, cost-of-ownership) remains applicable to enterprise management vendors; specific product references dated.
prescience_rationale: Strong prediction that Tivoli's 1996 product set would not be IBM's product three years later is partially verified — IBM Tivoli was repeatedly rearchitected and eventually subsumed into broader IBM portfolios.

title: Competing for the IBM AS/400 Market: A Report to the Management of Sun Microsystems
author: Peter Kastner
abstract: Aberdeen Group's formal deliverable to Sun Microsystems assessing Sun's opportunity to compete for the $10.27B IBM AS/400/iSeries market in 2002. The report sizes the addressable displacement opportunity at $1.87B across new customers, consolidation, enterprise applications, and Linux-lever upgrades. Recommendations focus on a partner-led strategy mirroring IBM's, Linux as the upgrade lever, and Europe as the key battleground SMB market. The analysis assumes flat 2002 iSeries revenues and identifies both positive factors (Regatta transition, Linux, consolidation trends) and negative factors (installed base inertia, IBM Global Services, lease renewal cycle closing).
importance_rationale: Defining Aberdeen RAMP deliverable for a marquee Sun engagement at a pivotal moment (post-dot-com, pre-Sun decline) proposing a $1.87B addressable market attack plan on IBM's midrange flagship.
relevance_rationale: RAMP methodology, addressable-market decomposition, and partner-channel analysis remain useful templates; specific 2002 competitive dynamics are historical.
prescience_rationale: Core recommendation — that Sun could capture meaningful AS/400 displacement via Linux-lever and partner-led attack — was refuted: Sun never captured material iSeries displacement share before Oracle's 2010 acquisition of Sun; iSeries rebranded as IBM i and continues shipping on Power Systems.

title: Sun's AS/400 Market Opportunity — Aberdeen Presentation to Sun Microsystems
author: Peter Kastner
abstract: Companion executive presentation deck to the April 2002 Aberdeen-to-Sun iSeries market report. Summarizes AS/400 demographics (450,000 installed base, $10.27B revenue, 23% of IBM HW), competitive assessment of iSeries strengths and weaknesses, IBM's 2002 game plan, Sun's $1.87B addressable market across four segments (new customers $924M, consolidation $400M, applications $452M, upgrades $92M), and the recommended partner-led Linux-lever strategy. Two file variants (ppt and pdf) contain the same content; merged here.
importance_rationale: Executive-facing summary deck for the flagship Sun RAMP engagement; primary analytical content duplicates the main report.
relevance_rationale: Deck format remains useful as a presentation template; content tracks the main report.
prescience_rationale: Same predictions as the main report: refuted — Sun did not meaningfully displace iSeries before Oracle's 2010 acquisition.

title: World IT Spending 2002-2005: Timing the Recovery
author: Aberdeen Group
abstract: Aberdeen Group's April 2002 forecast of worldwide IT spending through 2005, modeling the post-dot-com recovery path across regions (North America, Europe, Latin America, Asia-Pacific, Middle East/Africa) and categories (hardware, software, services). The report projects a return to growth from the 2001 trough and serves as the demand-side companion to Aberdeen's Sun AS/400 RAMP engagement. Includes CAGR projections by customer size (Small 1-99, Medium 100-999, Large 1000+, Government).
importance_rationale: Aberdeen's 2002 annual IT-spending forecast — standard reference for sizing platform opportunities in Sun engagement context.
relevance_rationale: Regional and category frameworks still useful; forecast values dated.
prescience_rationale: Forecast a recovery from 2001 trough through 2005 — directionally verified: world IT spending resumed growth through mid-2000s though pace differed from forecast.

title: Top iSeries ISVs — IBM Business Partner Directory Appendix
author: Peter Kastner (compiler)
abstract: Appendix B from the Sun AS/400 RAMP engagement: a directory of top-tier IBM iSeries ISV partners, their classification (Developer Business Partner vs Premier Level vs Advanced Level), locations (US, Canada, UK, Malaysia), and their flagship iSeries-compatible solutions. Named partners include Alltel Information Services (Horizon Banking), DWL Incorporated (DWL Customer/Unifi), e-Business Exchange Pte, E3 Corporation (E3 Marketplace, E3CRISP), EFA Software Services (Equator), Geac Enterprise Solutions (System21), and Hyperion Solutions. Used to shortlist Sun's counter-partner targets.
importance_rationale: Inventory of key iSeries channel partners Sun would need to recruit; defining operational artifact of the RAMP engagement.
relevance_rationale: Partner list is historical; most named ISVs have been acquired or transformed.
prescience_rationale: Directory of contemporaneous facts, not predictions.

title: Sun AS/400 RAMP Supporting Data: IBM Revenue, iSeries Pricing Benchmarks, Installed Base, and US Business Size Statistics
author: Peter Kastner
abstract: Consolidated analytical dataset backing the Sun AS/400 RAMP engagement. Includes: (a) IBM 2002 hardware financials — $33.4B revenue, $24.1B cost, $9.3B gross profit, 27.7% GM; platform revenue breakdown (zSeries $8.35B, iSeries $7.68B, pSeries $5.01B, xSeries $5.01B); iSeries model-to-Sun-equivalent mapping (250→Ultra 10, 270→220R, 820→V880, 830→4800, 840→6800); regional iSeries revenue breakdowns; enterprise app category CAGRs 2001-2005 (CRM 22%, EAS 12%, SCM 23%, B2B 26%); and Aberdeen's addressable-market decomposition ($1.87B total). (b) Detailed iSeries pricing benchmarks (181-row Appendix C) with TPM-C, SAP SD, and Notes benchmark estimates for each SKU. (c) World IT spending forecast 1999-2005 by region. (d) U.S. Business Statistics 1999 by firm size for IBM customer-size modeling.
importance_rationale: Defining quantitative foundation for the Sun AS/400 RAMP; model-level pricing and benchmarks rare in the archival record.
relevance_rationale: 2002-vintage data; analytical frameworks (platform-share, addressable-market yield factor, segment-CAGR) remain reusable templates.
prescience_rationale: Addressable-market sizing ($1.87B) was the central prediction; refuted in subsequent Sun execution but framework was sound.

title: Letter to Craig Barrett on Intel's 'Next Billion' Initiative: Field Observations from Haiti and Brazil
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: Personal letter from Peter S. Kastner (Managing Director, Scott-Page Associates) to Dr. Craig Barrett (Chairman, Intel) sharing firsthand field observations from third-world personal computing deployments in Haiti and rural Brazil. Kastner reports that endemic AC power failures are the single worst threat to PC adoption, calling out a 20-PC classroom in Jacmel, Haiti that required a $25K infrastructure stack (diesel genset, AC, UPS). Additional observations: ClassMate/EePC/OLPC child laptops are of lower demand than desktops; Microsoft software is in high third-world demand for job-skill building; classroom PCs need a model that requires zero IT administration. Proposes an Intel-integrated PC-plus-UPS power supply for emerging markets.
importance_rationale: Direct Kastner communication with Intel's Chairman during Intel's 'Next Billion' initiative; embodies on-the-ground field research in Haiti and Brazil post-Aberdeen.
relevance_rationale: Framework for evaluating emerging-market compute deployment (power, skills, management) remains highly relevant; specific product references dated.
prescience_rationale: Predicted OLPC/child-laptop weakness in third-world demand vs desktops — verified directionally (OLPC program underperformed expectations; netbooks faded; adult/shared desktops outsold pure child-laptops).

title: BAPco SYSmark 2012: Why AMD Dropped Out and What the Benchmark Actually Measures
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: Kastner blog post defending BAPco's SYSmark 2012 benchmark against AMD's June 2011 withdrawal from the consortium. Reports Kastner's own test results on the AMD Fusion A8-3850 APU ('Llano', scored 91) versus Intel Sandy Bridge Pentium 840 (98), Core i3-2120 (127), and Core i5-2500 (166). Argues the benchmark reflects 20 years of real-workload modeling across six scenarios (Office, Media, Web, Data/Financial, 3D, System Management) and rejects AMD's framing. The post is a commercial-benchmarks topic deep-dive from Kastner's post-Aberdeen independent-analyst era.
importance_rationale: Captures the AMD-BAPco schism mid-2011 — a pivotal commercial-benchmarks governance event. Kastner applied BAPco's own test harness to the disputed hardware.
relevance_rationale: Benchmark-bias framework and SYSmark methodology remain reusable templates; specific 2011 CPUs and scores are historical.
prescience_rationale: Kastner implicitly defends SYSmark relevance; AMD continued to reject BAPco-style benchmarks for a decade; overall frame was contested, not validated.

title: On the Impact of Paul Otellini's CEO Years at Intel (Tech.pinions, Published Version)
author: Peter Kastner
abstract: Published Tech.pinions column by Peter Kastner assessing the 40-year Intel career and 8-year CEO tenure (2005-2013) of Paul Otellini, organized across four dimensions: technology (tick-tock model, multi-core, High-K metal gate, 3D tri-gate, performance-per-watt), growth (BRIC geographies, mobile, software via McAfee and Wind River, intelligent systems), competition (AMD retake, ARM mobile threat), and finance (revenue up 60% to ~$53B; dividend initiation). Notes the missed-iPhone cost to Intel and the new emerging devices division.
importance_rationale: Public contemporaneous Kastner assessment of a Fortune-100 CEO transition; triangulates with two preview blog drafts (copy-4, copy-5) showing Kastner's publication workflow.
relevance_rationale: Framework (tech/growth/competition/finance) remains reusable; Haswell/14nm references dated.
prescience_rationale: Prediction of Intel mobile competitiveness via Atom and Infineon — refuted: Intel exited smartphone modems in 2019 and never achieved meaningful Atom smartphone share. Tick-tock model succeeded through mid-2010s, then broke at 10nm.

title: How the IT Industry Changed Under Intel's Paul Otellini (Kastner Blog Preview)
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: Early draft/preview of Kastner's Intel-Otellini analysis, posted on his own blog 'Peter S. Kastner on Technology' on April 17, 2013 (11 days before the Tech.pinions publication). Organizes the 2004-2013 Otellini tenure around three challenges (technology, growth, competition) and 'right-hand turns' — Intel jargon for pivoting without reversing course. Key content overlaps with the published Tech.pinions version (related_study_ids=otellini-intel-techpinions-2013-917213).
importance_rationale: Captures the iterative Kastner publishing workflow — personal blog first, iterative revision, then published via Tech.pinions.
relevance_rationale: Draft-stage content framework remains useful; specific product references dated.
prescience_rationale: Same predictions as the Tech.pinions version: Intel mobile ambitions via Atom/Infineon refuted.

title: On the Impact of Paul Otellini's CEO Years at Intel (Kastner Blog Preview)
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: Second-draft Kastner blog preview of the Otellini analysis, posted April 19, 2013 — two days after the 'How the IT Industry Changed' preview (copy-4) and nine days before the Tech.pinions publication. Retitled to match the final Tech.pinions headline; content substantially similar to the April 17 draft. Preserves Kastner's iterative publishing workflow: blog-draft → blog-refinement → Tech.pinions publication. Related studies: otellini-preview-how-it-changed-2013-604cfb (earlier draft), otellini-intel-techpinions-2013-917213 (published version).
importance_rationale: Captures intermediate draft stage in Kastner's public publishing workflow.
relevance_rationale: Content largely overlaps with the final Tech.pinions version.
prescience_rationale: Same predictions as the Tech.pinions version.

title: Pulse Check: How Intel is Scaling to Meet the Decade's Opportunities
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: Kastner blog analysis of Intel's IA (Intel Architecture) scaling strategy — the 'silicon gene-splicing' approach of creating IA variants for every computing tier from embedded SoCs, Atom smartphones, Core desktops/notebooks, to Xeon datacenter and PHI HPC. Projects the 2010s TAM at nearly 1B processors per year, rising to 2B+ by decade end. Reviews Haswell/Baytrail SoCs, Avoton Atom for micro-servers, and challenges around software delivery, sub-10nm scaling, missed-iPhone, and mobile-vs-PC substitution dynamics. Includes a personal recollection of advising NCR and Sequent on 486-based servers in the 1990s.
importance_rationale: Kastner's most substantive publicly-posted Intel strategy piece from 2013 — IA-scaling continuum frame was widely cited.
relevance_rationale: IA-as-continuum framework is durable; specific product codenames (Haswell, Baytrail, Avoton) are historical.
prescience_rationale: Core IA-scaling prediction largely refuted for mobile (Intel exited smartphone silicon); partially verified for datacenter (Intel dominant through 2020 then lost share to AMD Epyc and ARM); HPC via PHI discontinued 2020.

title: Don't Fall for the Siren-Song Price of Under-powered Desktop PCs (Aberdeen Impact)
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: Aberdeen Group 'Impact' publication (Sept 10, 1998) in which Kastner warns IS executives against the 'siren song' of sub-$1,000 and Celeron-class corporate desktops. Argues that memory pressure from Windows 95/98/NT + Office 97 + Outlook 98 + IE4 + SNMP + anti-virus consumes 60MB virtual memory, making 32MB PCs obsolete. Recommends a 400 MHz Pentium II with 128 MB and a 17-inch monitor at $1,800-2,000 for a 3-year useful life, sized for Office 2000, IE 5, and Windows NT 5.0 (later Windows 2000). Introduces Aberdeen's 'Welded Case' model — buy extra PC power now to avoid labor-intensive upgrades.
importance_rationale: Defining 1998 Kastner-authored Aberdeen 'Impact' on PC procurement strategy; called the corporate PC RAM/upgrade economics correctly at the transition from Windows 95 to Windows 2000.
relevance_rationale: 'Welded Case' buy-up-the-stack framework remains applicable; specific 1998 PC specs are historical.
prescience_rationale: Predicted that software upgrades would inevitably consume excess capacity, making 32MB PCs obsolete — verified as Windows 2000 required 64MB minimum and Office 2000 demanded real horsepower.

title: Aberdeen 1996 DORS Buying Guide — aberdeen.com Marketing Page (Outbound Marketing)
author: Aberdeen Group
abstract: Aberdeen Group marketing landing page for the 1996 edition of the 'Distributed, Open Relational Database Management Systems Buying Guide' (DORS), captured by the Internet Archive on Oct 23, 1996. Marketing asset evaluating eight DORS suppliers (Computer Associates, IBM, Informix, Microsoft, Oracle, Progress, Software AG, Sybase) across five technology dimensions (Scalability, Distributed Technology, Open Technology, Toolkits, Next-Generation including Internet/Intranets, relational OLAP, universal servers, workgroup servers). Lists 11 benefits and 4 key buying benefits. Contact: Susan Rinehart, One Boston Place. Primary archival value as outbound-marketing artifact demonstrating Aberdeen's 1996 web presence, report-marketing copywriting style, and database-market framing.
importance_rationale: Rare surviving 1996 snapshot of Aberdeen's commercial web presence; outbound marketing artifact showing how Aberdeen sold flagship reports in the early web era.
relevance_rationale: Reflects 1996 database-market framing; suppliers list is historical (most acquired).
prescience_rationale: Marketing page; not a prediction document.

title: Aberdeen Advisory Access Service (AAS) — Retainer Sales Kit
author: Aberdeen Group
abstract: Aberdeen Group internal sales/marketing collateral for the Advisory Access Service (AAS) retainer offering. Documents client benefits, 3-tier deliverables (Core Access Deliverables for all clients, Six-Month Introductory AAS for new clients at $6K, Twelve-Month Multi-Day AAS), a full labor-rate pricing ladder from Associate ($10,200) through Executive ($21,000), sales scripts for cold-call prospecting, research-briefing close, and follow-up, plus a canonical objection-handling playbook distinguishing Aberdeen from Forrester/Gartner (subscription-style) by positioning Aberdeen as 'personal service that takes research and puts it into your company's exact context'.
importance_rationale: Operational ground truth for Aberdeen's commercial sales model — labor rates, packaging, positioning vs Forrester/Gartner, cold-call and close scripts. Rare internal sales artifact.
relevance_rationale: Analyst-relations retainer sales playbook remains directly applicable; specific $ amounts are dated.
prescience_rationale: Internal sales collateral; not a prediction document.

title: Aberdeen Group Corporate Background Deck (Outbound Marketing)
author: Aberdeen Group
abstract: Aberdeen Group corporate background slide deck (v9-19) used for outbound marketing — client meetings, new-hire orientation, and investor briefings. Three offices (Boston, Palo Alto, Amsterdam), aberdeen.com URL. Four-slide structure: (1) title; (2) Aberdeen's Market Positioning; (3) Aberdeen's Competitive Landscape; (4) business architecture diagram mapping IT Supplier Executives and Private Equity audiences through Services, Practice Areas, Surveys/Field Instruments, Emerging Market Intelligence, Webcasts/Conferences/Seminars, aberdeen.com aggregation, Supplier Meetings, e-Learning, Field Sales, and Marketing Consulting. Primary text extraction is sparse — the deck is diagram-heavy.
importance_rationale: Rare surviving Aberdeen corporate overview deck — captures the firm's self-positioning and offer architecture circa turn-of-the-century.
relevance_rationale: Firm-branding deck is now historical but architectural framing of services × audience remains a reusable model for analyst firms.
prescience_rationale: Corporate marketing deck; not a prediction document.

title: AMD's Acquisition of ATI: Upsetting the Equilibrium
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: Aberdeen Group Market Alert by Peter S. Kastner (VP Enterprise Integration Research) analyzing AMD's $5.4B acquisition of ATI Technologies. Argues the deal upsets the four-way competitive equilibrium among AMD, ATI, Intel, and nVidia, and predicts less innovation and higher prices in enterprise volume servers and workstations. Kastner specifies buyer guidance for 2007-2008 planning: no immediate changes to purchasing, plan to test Windows XP/Vista on dual-core in Q1 2007, monitor Intel integrated graphics under Vista workload, and expects HP to gain and Dell to lose most among top-tier OEMs given Dell's Intel-only heritage.
importance_rationale: Timely strategic assessment of a major semiconductor M&A deal by a named Aberdeen analyst with specific OEM winner/loser predictions (HP gains, Dell loses).
relevance_rationale: M&A integration analysis frameworks and OEM impact reasoning remain broadly applicable; specific AMD/ATI product details are dated.

title: Aberdeen: Dell dives deeper, wider into enterprise
author: Peter S. Kastner
abstract: Kastner-authored analyst column 'special to SearchCIO.com' (published on searchdatamanagement.techtarget.com). Reviews Dell's enterprise push via its partnerships with Oracle, EMC, Intel, and Microsoft following Dell's FY2003 $35.4B in sales. Details Dell's Oracle 9i reseller program extension to Europe and Asia, Dell/EMC CX200/CX400/CX600 storage program with petabytes sold to 2,500 new customers, and Dell's 46-70% TCO advantage running Intel+Windows vs Unix servers. Concludes Dell will gain share in OLTP, messaging, data warehouse, and file/print markets as the IT megatrend favors standards-based computing.
importance_rationale: Kastner's own byline at SearchCIO.com documenting Dell's enterprise inflection point in 2003 with specific partnership and TCO data.
relevance_rationale: Dell's standards-based enterprise strategy and Intel+Windows TCO arguments remain useful historical reference; specific product configs (CX200, PowerEdge 2650, Oracle 9i on Red Hat Advanced Server) are dated.

title: Aberdeen Group Company Profile (Post-Kastner Leadership Era)
author: Aberdeen Group corporate
abstract: Undated Aberdeen Group corporate company profile documenting the post-Kastner leadership era: James Bedard CEO, Stephen Gold President & CMO, Mark Vigoroso Chief Research Officer. Peter Kastner appears on the Board of Directors, listed as 'Director, Vericours, Inc.' -- the first canonical source documenting Kastner's Vericours role after his Aberdeen tenure. John Logan (Aberdeen co-founder) is listed as CEO of Obian, foreshadowing the Logan-Kastner path from Aberdeen through Obian. Date approximated to ~2008-2010 based on leadership composition. Profile touts 125,000+ research community, 260,000 leads delivered, research used by 90%+ of Fortune 1000.
importance_rationale: Primary organizational evidence of Kastner's post-Aberdeen role at Vericours, Inc. plus the Logan-to-Obian migration that shaped Kastner's later employment. Only known corporate artifact capturing this transition.
relevance_rationale: Historically important for the Kastner/Aberdeen/Obian/Vericours organizational archaeology; not directly applicable to current technology analysis.
prescience_rationale: Corporate profile, no predictions made.

title: Obian Advisory Board (Kastner as Aberdeen Senior VP, Pre-Obian Employment)
author: Obian Group corporate
abstract: Obian Group's advisory board page (obian.com/board.shtml) naming Peter Kastner as 'Senior VP of Research, Aberdeen Group' among its advisors. Date approximated to ~August 2004 based on the most recent featured article ('Obian Viewpoint 03Aug04'). Documents the pre-employment advisory relationship that preceded Kastner's later move to Obian. Other advisors include Barry Kadets (CIO Bacou-Dalloz), Bob Lutz (Director of IT, Timex), Tom McCoy (former CIO AIG), Lawrence Meador (former CIO Cigna P&C), Ken Schwaber (Advanced Development Methods), and Patrick Zilvitis (CIO Segway, former CIO Gillette).
importance_rationale: Organizational evidence of Kastner's pre-employment advisory relationship with Obian; complements the later Aberdeen Company Profile that documents the same Aberdeen-Obian-Kastner-Logan network.
relevance_rationale: Narrow value: useful only for Kastner-Obian employment archaeology. No current-day applicability.
prescience_rationale: Advisory-board listing, no predictions made.
